text:
1

I want to swim, she thought.

She wanted to strip off her shirt, skirt, and underwear and leave them in a heap at her feet, feel the softness of the water and the refraction of light on her bare skin, and keep moving through that soundless space wherever her heart led her. As the temperature of the water grew familiar against her skin, the boundary between herself and the world would blur, and weight, age, even sex would lose their meaning. If she tried to speak, every word would turn to bubbles and rise, higher and higher, smudging into the white distance before disappearing. Summer was almost over, so why did she feel that way?

That was it. The early-morning office before her was very like a deserted indoor pool.

Desks in neat rows as though divided by lane ropes, the pale-blue hush of an empty room like the surface of water. Even the smell of chlorine and toner was similar, both sharp enough to make her feel some memory was about to surface. Looking for a time and place where she could work without anyone interrupting her, where she could be alone, she had ended up here after all, in the office where she spent most of her day. This was the place where she was supposed to show herself at her most capable, and yet right now she could have shouted complete nonsense at the top of her lungs or broken into some ridiculous dance, and it would have been perfectly fine. Of course, she did no such thing.

It was simply not in her nature to raise her voice or dance.

The Food Products Sales Division, occupying half of the nineteenth floor of the Otemachi headquarters of Nakamaru Trading, Japan’s largest trading company, was completely empty between six and seven in the morning. It handled the classic business of a trading company: importing food from overseas and selling it to Japanese firms.

The fresh, still-unmarked air before her calmed Eriko Shimura’s mind and body, always bent on reaching conclusions. It fell to the first one in, so she switched on the copier.

Ever since she was a girl, she had liked getting out ahead of things. Her father, an old hand at the same company, had taught her that staying one step ahead was one of the essential qualities of a trading company man. Her parents, with whom she lived in an apartment in Setagaya, seemed unhappy that their only daughter was never at the breakfast table, but by her eighth year at the company they had stopped saying anything.

Even now, at thirty, Eriko had no intention of leaving her parents’ home. Without her mother’s help, there was no way she could keep herself so impeccably put-together, stay in good health, and keep working. And if she moved out, her mother too would probably feel stifled by the daily routine of just the two of them, her taciturn father and herself.

No matter how early she came in, she had less than forty minutes to herself. It was the kind of workplace where most of her colleagues showed up an hour before the official 8:30 start, checking email with convenience-store breakfasts in hand. Threading her way between the fifty-odd desks, she sank her Tod's pumps one after the other into the carpet, the leather polished by her mother until even the outlines of the fluorescent lights reflected crisply in it, and headed straight for her station. Half the staff in the Food Products Sales Division were permanent employees, but among them the only women were Eriko and section chief Sayako Yotsuba, a decade her senior at the company. The temp workers and contract employees, by contrast, were all women, and they invariably came in at the last minute before the workday began.

The convenience-store bag holding that morning's breakfast and the Nikkei rustled against her beige pleated skirt. She sat down at her desk and turned on her computer. The startup sound resonated so low it seemed to shake the floor beneath her feet, tracing an invisible arc as it slowly spread all around.

This was the only moment when she was allowed not to think, not to do anything. It might even have been the most restful part of her day. Until the orderly desktop appeared on the screen, she savored that small freedom. Now that her annual income had long since topped ten million yen, Eriko could say with confidence that the single most valuable thing in the world was time. Given enough time, most things could somehow be managed. Because everyone rushed through their days, in the modern world anything on which a great deal of time had been spent, whether a project, a product, or a work, was highly valued for that fact alone. Proper table manners, letters that opened with a gentle seasonal greeting and ran to at least three pages, makeup so light it looked like bare skin, well-maintained leather goods: the admiration they drew was respect for the time that had gone into them.

To clear a space for breakfast, she first took out an alcohol wipe and scrubbed the desk clean with brisk, squeaking strokes. She carefully wiped between the keys of the keyboard and the mouthpiece of the phone receiver as well.

Even if they mocked her as neurotic, unless she did this she could not feel that the portion of this overlarge space allotted to her was really her station. She tackled the pile of routing documents, skimming them one by one. The work moved along several times faster than it did during office hours, with people constantly coming and going and the phones ringing without pause. Time could be made, if you were clever about it. It was the first thing her immediate supervisor had taught her when she started working. A few minutes later, when the pile of papers had finally disappeared, Eriko, feeling like rubbing her hands together, at last drew the convenience-store bag to the center of the desk.

She took out a paper carton of cafe au lait. Then a wrapped sweet bun, a new product called karinto melon bread. She had checked two convenience stores in her neighborhood that morning and found both sold out; it was a precious find, one she had finally tracked down at the shop inside the subway station. Even though she knew there was no way it contained anything good for her, she still found herself turning it over to check the ingredients list. Maybe that was an occupational hazard.

Mitsuzaki, a major bread manufacturer, was one of the grain division's clients. Artificial sweeteners, preservatives, food coloring. Even now, at her age, there was a faintly illicit pleasure in secretly eating the sort of thing her natural-food-loving mother would have hated. And it cost only ninety-eight yen. Back in middle school and high school, some of her classmates used to buy sweet buns like this at the school store and fill their stomachs with them. She had been grateful, of course, for her mother's homemade lunches, but Eriko had envied those huge, garishly colored buns with an almost painful intensity. She had felt that the bodies of people her own age, so easily satisfied by such carefree snacks, held inner lives just as easygoing and unconfined.

She let the row of bookmarked sites spill open, moved the cursor to the URL of her favorite blog, and with a click dove into a pocket of time that belonged only to the two of them. As her eyes ran down the screen, she bit into a melon bread nearly the size of her face. The cookie crust cracked apart with a crunch, and butter, dark sugar, and melon flavor flooded out. She was right. The balance of salt and sweetness was not bad, but it tasted unmistakably cheap. Even so, taken together with the words flowing across the LCD screen, it tasted delicious in a way that seeped through her.

"The idea of putting karinto and melon bread together is just dumb, right? I spotted it at the convenience store and cracked up. But if you really chew that oily, crunchy dough, with its faint soy-sauce note, kind of like mitarashi, a fresh melon smell opens up like coming out of a tunnel. I'm addicted to it right now. Dumb taste. Dumb price. If you make lunch out of this kind of 'dumb food,' a lot of things get easier. There's something soothing about going easy on life. Oh, I'm not recommending it, though."

Going easy on life. That's a good phrase, Eriko thinks. For her, life had always been something to face in deadly earnest, something to wrestle with. She was drifting in a haze of faint accomplishment and the stale heaviness of oil spreading through her chest when a voice dropped from above her.

"Eating high-school-girl food, Shimura. You'll get fat."

Whatever the situation, Yasuyuki Sugishita, her colleague on the fisheries team, had a way of speaking that made the other person first search for their own fault. While they were still reflexively checking themselves over, he would simply keep pressing ahead at his own pace. That manner seemed to fend off criticism itself. Though he was hardly a perfectionist, Eriko had never seen a superior scold him or even caution him. In a sales division full of sports-club types, Sugishita stood out: a scrawny build, a face that was well-formed but a little long, a narrow chin, thin, sallow lips. He had an unusual background, having studied architecture in graduate school, so although he had joined the company in the same cohort as Eriko, he was two years older.

"Morning. Oh, this bread? It was featured on a blog. I finally managed to get one."

Disappointed that her time alone had ended sooner than she had expected, Eriko pointed at the computer screen on instinct. Sugishita braced a hand on her desk and leaned in close. Ah, too close. She thought that, but not enough to make her pull away. The pungent smell of the styling product Sugishita always used stung her nose.

"Huh. A housewife blog? You're single, Shimura. What's so interesting about that?"

'Ohyou's Hopeless Housewife Diary' was a blog she had been reading almost every day for the past two years. A bare-bones homepage. Long posts with hardly any paragraph breaks. The occasional photos, taken on a cell phone, were not very good, to be charitable. Even so, it was always in the top thirty on one site's ranking of popular housewife blogs. Maybe what set it apart was that it had none of the 'haters' that seem to come with housewife blogs.

"I know, but she isn't all that housewife-housewife. She's relaxed in exactly the right way. Her eating habits and housekeeping are pretty slapdash, but somehow there's still this sense of cleanliness, or maybe restraint. And she's the same age as me, so I feel close to her."

Ohyou lived in a Tokyo apartment with her husband, who managed a supermarket. Apparently they had no plans for children for the time being. They did not seem especially well-off, yet there was no sign that she worked part-time. Even though she was a full-time housewife, some days she cooked and some days she did not. She often met her husband at a family restaurant or a conveyor-belt sushi place and ate dinner there. He never seemed to reproach her for it, and the two of them seemed extremely happy together. But neither did she go out of her way to advertise how close they were. Eriko also liked her style in calling him not 'danna-san' or 'so-and-so-kun,' but 'the Dark Lord.'

'I want kids, but we never even get to the making-them part! Sex isn't so bad once it's started, but getting to the point of doing it is such a pain, isn't it? Hey, Dark Lord, you reading this?'

She would toss out something that risqué as if it were nothing.

It was exhilarating when she flatly wrote that she never saved points at supermarkets or drugstores, never used coupons; that was her principle.

'Those guys are stealing our precious time (mad). Even if you keep fussing around and squirreling them away bit by bit, in the end it's what, maybe five hundred yen? When you think about the trouble of digging a point card out of your wallet and handing it over every time you go to the register just for that, it's way more refreshing to just ditch the whole lot. If you added up all the time that hassle takes over an entire lifetime, it might come to ten hours or so. We're not rich or anything, so maybe I'm losing out like crazy, but it's such a pain, so whatever~~. The minute I threw them all out, wow, my wallet was insanely light! So light~'

Eriko had always hated the way her mother stuffed her wallet full of point cards and coupons. Her father had become the president of a Nakamaru Trading subsidiary fairly early on, and even though they lived comfortably enough, it saddened her to see her mother doing whatever the sellers wanted for the sake of a mere hundred or two hundred yen. Her mother looked easygoing at first glance, but she was easily pressured and couldn't stand her ground. She was the type who couldn't throw away anything she was given and kept it forever. Maybe it was all the more unpleasant because she had that same side to her. Ohyou probably knew by instinct that time came before money. That was why she could move through her own time freely, with nothing tying her down.

"What's so interesting about a diary like this? It has nothing to do with your life. It's all so domestic."

For some reason, it stung a little that Sugishita seemed completely unmoved, and she began to speak faster.

"But that's what's so good about her, isn't it, how real she feels? I like how she doesn't seem like she's forcing anything. I don't know, I just think she's kind of cute. I love how time in it just flows, full and slow."

"You get a kick out of making fun of some idle housewife? Can't stand it, women. Always needing to find some woman beneath them so they can feel better."

Though she was used to this kind of remark from the men in the office, it still snagged. When she had first joined the company, she had been shocked by how openly contemptuous of women the office culture was, but by now she had given up. Let it slide. Don't rise to every little jab. There was no malice in it; astonishingly, they simply gave no thought to what they said, so taking it seriously only left her worse off. Make an issue of it, and she would only be the one who ended up looking foolish. With her father's backing, and as a permanent employee, Eriko would be treated as an equal as long as she remembered to keep herself more impeccably put-together than anyone else and work as hard as the men. There was no point getting irritated over petty everyday turns of phrase. She tried not to let her good-humored front slip.

"Hmm, that's not it. Usually housewife blogs have that element of showing off, don't they? Showing how happy they are, how well they keep house, how good they are at cooking. That desire to be understood is so strong. But she..."

The phone on the desk rang, and their conversation was cut short. When she picked up, it was a tuna dealer calling from California. This was the time of day when calls from America came in one after another.

"May I have your name, please? When shall I have him return your call?"

After telling the caller that the person in charge was away from his desk, Eriko turned back to Sugishita.

It was frustrating not to be able to explain it properly. She liked the way Ohyou could seem almost to be playing dumb and yet think so sharply. Her words flowed pleasantly through her chest. Her language was gentle, but it conveyed intelligence, and it would never hurt anyone. She lived in sheer idleness, yet was neither ashamed of it nor trying to make it mean something. There was no sign that not having children made her anxious, either. More than anything, it was full of turns of phrase that stayed with her and offhand little ideas that made her want to copy them.

For instance, on muggy days she did the housework in a swimsuit, then pulled a dress over her head and went off to dive into the local public pool. At the library, all she ever borrowed was Ishida Sen. Just to read the complete run of Kubo Kiriko's *Gohan in a Bucket*, she kept going to a halfhearted neighborhood cafe that was obviously only kept open for tax reasons, deliberately ordering the menu items that took the most work. She and her husband went out for karaoke and sang Western songs nonstop. They ordered pizza and watched whatever Hollywood movie was on television. She went from convenience store to convenience store hunting for Rich Corn Potage-flavored Gari Gari Kun.

She was drawn to the way she spent whole days doing nothing in particular, without looking for meaning in it, without making plans, simply following her whims like a cat. When had she last spent her days like that? It was so long ago she could no longer remember. Just reading her blog loosened, if only a little, the nerves that nightly business dinners and market research kept taut.

Whatever the circumstance, Eriko always tried, first of all, not to waste time. Even away from work, she put effort into beauty, research, and study, and of course she also valued rest and sleep. She was confident that everything she did led to some kind of benefit. She had no doubt that she was better today than she had been yesterday. And yet, perhaps since around the time she turned thirty, there were moments when a sharp sting would catch behind her nose. At times she felt as though there was not enough oxygen, as though she could fling both arms around and touch nothing at all. She could not help taking a deep breath, telling herself over and over that she was all right, and counting off one by one her strengths, her growth, her contributions to the company. When she absolutely could not calm down, she would adopt more humility than necessary to draw praise out of someone, and only then regain her composure. Even so, no matter how she struggled, she could feel a little more possibility wearing away with every day's end and every morning, leaving her less and less able to correct her course. Was this anxiety something that came with age? In a few more years, would she simply get used to it? Whenever, in some unguarded instant, she caught a trace of the same anxiety in another woman, she could not stop the relief and twisted pleasure from seeping out.

There was no trace of that kind of anxiety in Ohyou.

More than anything, what drew her in was that the blog featured almost no one but her husband, and no female friends at all. Among housewife blogs whose authors tended to show off their circles of friends, it stood out. Eriko secretly hoped that perhaps Ohyou knew how to tame the same kind of hang-up she had.

She was saved by the fact that even a woman like her had no friends. Even someone who, by some miraculous balance, never rubbed people the wrong way might still be unable to make friends because of the slightest mistiming. Maybe having no female friends was not such a shameful defect. Maybe it was not a defect at all, but simply one variation in human relationships.

"I'm a thirty-year-old housewife living with my husband in a rundown apartment in Tokyo. My favorite thing to eat is the engawa at Nikkori Zushi, the ninety-eight-yen-a-plate conveyor-belt sushi place down the street."

Sugishita, who had seemed uninterested, finally leaned forward when he read Ohyou's profile.

"Huh. So engawa at conveyor-belt sushi is her favorite. If she knows the engawa at those dirt-cheap conveyor-belt places is halibut, not flounder, and that's the screen name she picked, that's pretty pointed."

Halibut can top two hundred kilos and grow to nearly three meters long, so the engawa you get from them is huge. When they are hauled out of the water they thrash so violently that in Alaska they apparently kill them with shotguns. Eriko felt as if she herself had been praised, and wanted to throw back her shoulders.

"Come to think of it, Sugishita. You were in charge of Russian halibut last year, weren't you? And weren't we doing business with Center Village Holdings too?"

That big restaurant chain was supposed to have Nikkori Zushi under its umbrella. At Sugishita's smirk, Eriko clapped her hands before she knew it.

"Wow... amazing. It all connects. So Ohyou-san's favorite food is what you handle, Sugishita."

Moved, Eriko looked around the office, where it was just the two of them. As she tracked the spread of numbers and goods, there were moments when the world suddenly felt smaller, near enough to grasp in her own hands, and she loved those moments more than anything. At some point the sun had climbed high, and the late-August light was slanting in. The carpet, turned white and fuzzy from being cleaned so often, had begun to give off a sun-warmed smell.

Working at a trading company, you sometimes couldn't help seeing unexpected depth in ordinary scenes. This cafe au lait, this bread, this newspaper. Where their raw materials had come from, been grown or raised, how they had been distributed and processed, and come to be here now. If you narrowed your eyes, each one's story seemed to spread out. Both Eriko and Sugishita had that kind of ability to see through things.

Japan consumed around 6.52 million tons of seafood. Forty percent of it came from imports. Many of those imports were fish with no names of their own, species used as substitute fish or mislabeled fish. Since being assigned to the fisheries team, Eriko had learned to tell at a glance what a processed fish really was, whatever the label said.

"It does make you feel a little guilty toward consumers. 'Engawa' is just the name of the cut, so it's not exactly a lie, but still."

"But if you stop and think about it, there's no way flounder or plaice engawa costs under a hundred yen. Consumers probably have some vague sense of that too."

"At this point it's less a food fish than some kind of alien, isn't it? Kind of scary."

"And everyone's eating that alien as nigiri, going, 'Mmm, delicious.' So everyone's happy, right? The 'fish' in a fish burger, the 'white fish' in white fish meuniere. Once you start asking what kind of fish it is and where it came from, there's no end to it. There really are a lot of things in this world you don't need to know."

For all Sugishita's brusque, distancing manner, put him in a negotiation and he could pour on the charm to a startling degree, every word smooth and easy. He gave the impression of being high-strung, but in fact he was the son of a kimono shop in Kawagoe that had been in the family for three generations.

"And you're on Nile perch starting this month, right, Shimura? If she's that into conveyor-belt sushi and family restaurants, some fish from one of your deals is bound to end up in her mouth sooner or later."

Eriko flipped through the file she had inherited from her predecessor. Nile perch was a freshwater fish in the order Perciformes, family Latidae, genus Lates. It could grow to two meters and two hundred kilos. Native to Africa, it lived in lakes and rivers. It was a carnivore so ferocious you would never guess it from its mild, bland taste. When it was introduced into Lake Victoria, it drove more than two hundred endemic species of small cichlids to extinction. Its mild, unobtrusive flesh made it easy for Japanese consumers to accept, and in the 1990s it had even been sold as "sea bass" or "white sea bass," but lately ninety percent of it was exported to Europe and North America, and you hardly ever saw it. For the past several months, Eriko had been working to open up that market again and get the facilities on site into shape. The more she learned about the fish called Nile perch, the more helplessly it drew her in.

"Nile perch just have such sheer vitality, don't they?"

"Yikes, it's a carnivore, right? No telling what it eats. Scary, scary. Oh, right. Apparently you should watch this documentary called Darwin's Nightmare. It sounds like it shows Nile perch wrecking the ecosystem."

"I keep thinking I should watch it sometime, but I never have the time. And until this next round of talks is over, it may not be the kind of thing I want in my head.... If they hadn't been released into the lake by human hands, Nile perch might have gone their whole lives without ever realizing how vicious they were. I feel a little sorry for them. Oh, and apparently they're popular as aquarium fish too. They're silver, and very beautiful. They have a kind of lonely look to them...."

"You can see them at that new aquarium in Shinjuku, right? Want to go sometime?"

At his muttered invitation, Eriko said neither yes nor no, and pretended to be absorbed in the blog again. She knew perfectly well that Sugishita felt a faint fondness for her. Apparently he had imposed a rule on himself that if he put it into plain words, he lost, and his face had taken on an expression strained with wanting and embarrassment. Even this needling was probably his way of showing affection. Perhaps he did not want to admit she had evaded him; he hurriedly dragged the conversation back on course.

"Well, maybe she's okay as long as you're only reading the blog. No way it'd work if you actually met her."

"That's not true. I feel like Ohyou and I could be good friends."

"Yeah, right. You don't even have any women friends."

She felt her fingertips go suddenly cold. No matter how much effort she made, no matter how completely she concentrated every nerve on that one point, she still "didn't look like a woman who had women friends." Her heart thudded at that unalterable fact. In contrast to her shock, Sugishita's words carried a sticky sexual undertone. A woman who couldn't get into women's circles stirred a faint lust in men. There was even a trace of pride there, as if he had discovered a serious failing in the opposing camp without dirtying his own hands. Pretending not to notice, she made sure her voice sounded utterly casual.

"Well, that's true enough. Since I started working, I've drifted away from my college group too. Even when they invite me to girls' get-togethers, I never have time to go."

By taking his point for the moment, she could keep the upper hand instead; it was a tactic she had learned in business. No such group existed, and no one had ever invited her to a girls' get-together. Sometimes Eriko found it almost dazzling, the brazenness and childishness of grown women who called themselves "girls" with a straight face.

"You don't go to lunch with the temps or the others from your year in other departments either, Shimura."

Though he never said it outright, Sugishita was apparently secretly dating a temp worker named Maori Takasugi. That might have been why he knew so much about the relationships among the women in the office.

"Oh, that. My schedule never matches up with the others from my year, and going out to eat with temp workers takes some tact. A lot of them want to keep it under a thousand yen, so you can't just invite them without thinking."

Unable to say that of course it was because no one invited her, Eriko made a point of keeping her tone bright.

"I guess it's true, I've probably always been bad at getting along with other girls. I mean, not to say it myself, but I'm that high-strung model-student type, right? I was always getting saddled with class-rep jobs and organizer roles, and before I knew it I'd be by myself. I never really got those invisible rules girls have, or whatever you call them, and somehow I always seem to end up out of step without even realizing it."

She was talking surprisingly fast, even to herself. Please, she prayed. Don't ask anything more. Beyond this was unknown territory. She wasn't sure she could keep the cracks from showing.

"Well, a pretty woman like you, who can do anything without even trying, probably makes women jealous. Women are scary when they're jealous."

Sugishita's eyes narrowed almost fondly. Did he think he was complimenting her? He was probably comparing her with Maori Takasugi. Maori Takasugi, bad at her job, talkative, and plump and fleshy. He was probably just toying with her. And yet Maori was always surrounded by the other women in the office, and Eriko envied her that.

Without trying. Sugishita had probably never even thought about how much time and money went into this skin without a single blemish, this glossy hair. All at once, Eriko found herself wondering what kind of lotion Ohyou used. Some cheap stuff from a drugstore bargain bin? Or maybe, unexpectedly, she was into organic cosmetics. Eriko wanted to try the same thing. As she dug through old posts, her lips were moving.

"Still, it looks like this Ohyou lives near me."

"Huh. Seriously?"

"Look, this organic cafe she mentions here, Giselle. It's one stop away, and I know it well because my mother was involved in getting it started years ago. And all the fast-food places and chain restaurants Ohyou likes are right by my nearest station too. I mean, they're all the kind of places you can find anywhere, but still."

"Knowing all that stuff is a little gross. To her, you'd look like a stalker."

The word jolted her. It had never once occurred to Eriko before. She turned and stared at Sugishita.

"What? What's that supposed to mean? I'm just reading her blog. I just happened to realize we might live near each other, that's all it is, isn't it?"

"But you're copying that Ohyou person's eating habits, checking up on her day-to-day life, and now it turns out you live in the same neighborhood. Even if you don't mean anything by it, from her side it'd be a little creepy. Don't overdo it."

He broke off, turned on his heel, and went back to his desk. Employees were beginning to trickle in. He must have come in this early because he wanted to get her alone without Maori Takasugi noticing. Was it conceited of her to think that? A year had passed since she broke up with her last boyfriend, but she still did not feel lonely. Someday she wanted to get married, but for now shuttling between home and the office was all she could manage.

Had Ohyou ever imagined an office worker like her, in an office this early, nibbling the same bread while reading her blog? After all, Eriko had a presentiment that somewhere Ohyou must be aware of her, aware of her as a person. At first glance, Ohyou put on a casual air, but perhaps she was tucking messages for all sorts of people into what she wrote. You're fine as you are. Take it easy. What's wrong with being on your own?

As if cherishing another self in the mirror, Eriko carefully reread Ohyou's entries.

2

Shoko Maruo stopped updating her blog on her phone and looked up.

Staring at the denim flapping from the balcony of the apartment building across the way, she thought that laundry looked ready to fall. Must be some young woman's. Shoko was thirty now; there was no way she could pull off skinny jeans like that. She used to stand on the department store sales floor in pants so tight they nearly cut off her circulation. Standing for more than ten hours a day had been routine. There had even been times when she only got two days off a month. What would the Shoko she had been then think if she saw the life she had now? She might, unexpectedly, envy it.

In any case, she might have arrived a little too early. Giselle, the organic cafe she loved, was a fifteen-minute walk from her apartment building. Maybe because it was Saturday, she noticed a few women on their own who looked much like her. She popped the rest of the food from the big plate into her mouth one thing after another - konnyaku and chicken liver in mustard mayonnaise, coleslaw with bitter melon, a brown-rice ball with sesame and yukari, an omelet with aosa - and polished it all off at a speed that made even her shake her head. It had been a long time since she had eaten such a balanced meal, with this many vegetables, and just as long since she had made one for Kensuke. Once again she felt like a failure as a housewife. Tonight, at least, she would stew some hijiki. No... just imagining it was a hassle, so maybe she should have them wrap something up for her here. She did not want to wash dishes. More than anything, Shoko hated kitchen cleanup.

She met Kensuke, the manager of a supermarket that specialized in imported groceries, through a part-time job and married him three years ago. In principle, she tried to be frugal so they could live on Kensuke's salary alone, but she hated the thought of letting thrift cramp her whole way of thinking. The occasional little wasteful purchase was no great matter; she could always draw on the savings she had built up while she was single. These days, the affiliate ads on her blog brought in a little money too. No wedding ceremony, no rings, no trips abroad. Of course no house, either. They did without major luxuries so they would not have to be petty about the small ones. Rather than fight over the dishes, they chose to eat out somewhere cheap. It was a promise they had made when they filed their marriage papers.

Doutor or a family restaurant would honestly have been fine again today, but the person she was meeting was one of those media types, after all, so she picked the chicest cafe she knew. The wood-toned interior always made her feel at ease. Hashimoto, the part-timer who had come to clear her plate, suddenly bent down close.

"Shoko-saan. Thanks for featuring our new fall menu on your blog."

"Whoa, too close, too close. You scared me."

His breath against her ear tickled. Hashimoto was twenty-five, the same age as her younger brother, which made him easy to talk to. When she gave him a light smack, she felt beneath his shirt a taut bicep that did not match his lanky build. The firmness that nearly bounced her hand away was nothing like the soft, squishy upper arm of her husband, who had started gaining weight again lately.

"On days your blog mentions us, we get a few more customers than usual."

For Shoko, who preferred family restaurants and fast food, Giselle was one of the few independent cafes she frequented. Since taking a short part-time job there over the New Year's holidays earlier that year, she had gotten to know the staff, and even after leaving she still came in once or twice a month as a customer. She had turned down their invitation to work there as a contract employee. If it became her workplace, the relationships would inevitably become a hassle, and she could already see the result: she would not be able to come here like this anymore.

Shoko lowered her gaze to her phone again. Just tracing through the comments on her blog was enough to make time disappear. Almost none of them condemned her sloppiness. Most were supportive instead: 'This is reassuring,' 'Makes me feel like this is okay.' Plenty of people also posted links to products or places they recommended. She was happy to feel she had finally found a place where she belonged, but when people said nothing but kind things to her, part of her could not help feeling she was deceiving her readers. Even so, there was something addictive about how good those approving comments felt, and she would spend hours rereading them. Maybe it was like soaking in the lukewarm carbonated bath at a big public bathhouse. When the stress was that low, it became hard to tell when it was time to get out.

Online or off, she wanted as many places as possible where she could feel at ease, and as many people as possible she could share a smile with. Even after eight years in Tokyo, she still couldn't get used to the city. When she was working, she had been too busy to go out and have fun, and after her health broke down, she'd become a complete homebody. Just as the bell over the door rang, a woman in a suit, around forty or so, appeared. She noticed Shoko right away and made straight for her.

"Ohyou-san... are you Shoko Maruo? Nice to meet you. My name is Satoko Hanai, from Shumeisha's Third Book Editorial Department."

Shoko hurried to her feet, bowed, and took the card. Satoko Hanai had neat features and a decent figure, but her dry skin and the dark circles under her eyes made it hard to shake the impression that she was worn out.

"Well, this is a surprise. You're much cuter and more charming than I'd imagined, Ohyou-san."

Stopping short of "beautiful" was just the kind of carefulness Shoko associated with elites. I'm being appraised. She could feel a sensor that had been asleep all this time click back on. She was wearing a washed-out T-shirt and a pair of chinos Kensuke had worn back when he was thinner, with hardly any makeup on. She knew well enough it wasn't an outfit for a first meeting. Ever since quitting the apparel company, she'd lost almost all interest in the clothes she'd once been so obsessed with buying. She had never been striking to begin with. Her figure had no curves to speak of, her eyes were monolidded, and freckles stood out against her pale skin. Her lips curled back, and she had always been self-conscious about the way her gums showed when she smiled.

And yet there were certain women who, for some reason, seemed almost to have agreed among themselves to loathe Shoko.

She fawns on men.

That was what they said. But she didn't. It was only that the ordinary consideration and care women probably showed one another came easily to her when the other person was a man. There were plenty of moments when, faced with a woman, she had no idea what to say, but with men she instinctively knew how to be. Maybe it was because her parents had divorced, and she'd grown up with her father, surrounded by brothers. She wasn't calculating by nature, and it wasn't as if she'd had all that much romantic experience either. It felt unfair to be judged that way simply because she could relax around men. Out of that wariness, she had come to make a point of acting rougher than necessary around other women.

Satoko Hanai ordered coffee, then set several magazines for housewives and a few collections of housewife blogs published by Shumeisha on the table and began explaining this and that. To be honest, Shoko wasn't especially eager. She had never been good with people expecting things of her. Just reading those housewife-blogger posts, with their happiness on display and that stifling heat coming off them, was exhausting. She was the sort of person who wanted to avoid anything troublesome whenever possible. Paying no attention to Shoko's mood, Satoko kept right on talking, the words coming fast.

“These days, housewife bloggers have carved out all kinds of niches, but your selling point is that natural, unforced quality. You never tell your readers how they should be or how they ought to live, and that’s extremely appealing. In a climate where all sorts of values are being accepted, plenty of housewives are still struggling to find their footing. And not just housewives — I’m sure a great many people respond to the way you stay true to your own pace without wavering. There’s nothing pushy about you, and I think you have the kind of appeal that would land even with single working women.”

“Mm... But even if a diary like that were turned into a book, who’d read it...”

“Exactly, so what if we reworked the diary and shaped it into essays? I’d love for you to write a lifestyle guide — the perspective of a down-to-earth woman who doesn’t try too hard.”
